does: This paper documents OpenAI's updates to its Model Spec with new Under-18 Principles designed specifically for teen users of ChatGPT. The work articulates concrete behavioral guidelines grounded in developmental science that specify how the model should interact with users under eighteen, emphasizing safe and age-appropriate responses. The update strengthens existing safety guardrails by clarifying expected model behavior in higher-risk situations such as mental health crises, self-harm, substance use, and other sensitive topics where teens may seek guidance. The paper presents the principles as an evolution of OpenAI's broader safety framework, translating developmental psychology research into actionable constraints that govern model outputs.
connects: This work builds on the tradition of AI safety research focused on responsible model deployment, extending prior work on value alignment and behavioral specifications for large language models. It responds to growing concerns about AI systems interacting with minors, drawing on established child safety frameworks and developmental psychology literature that articulates how adolescents differ from adults in decision-making, emotional regulation, and vulnerability to harm. The Model Spec itself represents an evolution of prior specification-based approaches to AI alignment, building on methods for formalizing expected behavior beyond training data. The update also connects to broader industry and regulatory discourse around age-appropriate AI systems, child online protection standards, and the emerging field of AI ethics focused on vulnerable populations.
enables: This framework establishes a replicable methodology for incorporating developmental science into AI system specifications, enabling other organizations to implement similar age-stratified safety measures. The work creates a foundation for more nuanced interaction models that can dynamically adapt to user age and developmental stage, potentially informing how future systems recognize and appropriately respond to teenage users. By articulating specific behavioral commitments in high-risk domains, the paper enables measurable evaluation of teen safety in AI systems and provides a template for translating scientific understanding into deployed safeguards. This approach could support regulatory compliance with emerging child protection requirements while pointing toward more sophisticated personalization that respects developmental differences without limiting access.
